meta.title: 2.1 CSS Is Just Instructions
meta.description: Selectors pick the target. Properties describe the change. That is all CSS does.

blocks.prompt: You see this CSS code: `h1 { color: blue; }`. Without any prior knowledge of CSS, what do you think this does?

blocks.content: ## CSS Is Just Instructions Every CSS instruction follows the same pattern: ```css selector { property: value; } ``` That is it. Every piece of CSS you will ever see is some variation of this pattern. Let's break it down: - **Selector** — *which* elements to change (the target) - **Property** — *what* to change about them - **Value** — *how* to change it ### Reading CSS out loud The single best trick for reading CSS is to translate it into a sentence: ```css h1 { color: blue; } ``` > "For all `h1` elements, set the color to blue." ```css p { font-size: 18px; margin-bottom: 12px; } ``` > "For all paragraphs, set the font size to 18 pixels and the bottom margin to 12 pixels." Once you can do this, you can read any CSS. The selectors and properties might get more specific, but the pattern never changes. ### Where CSS lives CSS can appear in three places: **1. External stylesheet** (most common in real projects) ```html ``` The CSS lives in a separate `.css` file. This is how most real websites work — keeps things organized. **2. Style tag** (in the HTML file) ```html ``` The CSS is written directly in the HTML document, inside `

blocks.question: How confident are you that you could read a CSS rule like `.card { background-color: #f0f0f0; border-radius: 8px; }` and explain what it does in plain English?
blocks.answer: This says: "For all elements with the class `card`, set the background color to light gray (#f0f0f0) and round the corners by 8 pixels." If you got the gist — targeting a class and changing how it looks — you are on track. The specific property names become familiar with exposure.

options: A) Sets a black background and white text on elements with the class `hero`
B) Creates a new HTML element called `hero` with black and white colors
C) Removes the background from all hero images on the page
problems.explanation: The `.hero` selector targets elements with the class `hero`. `background-color: #000` is black, and `color: #fff` is white. Read it as: "For all elements with class hero, make the background black and the text white."

problems.question:
A page has this CSS in a stylesheet: ```css h1 { color: red; } ``` But in the HTML, the heading has: ```html

Welcome

``` What color will the heading be?
options: A) Red, because the stylesheet was loaded first
B) Green, because inline styles override stylesheet rules
C) Both colors will blend together
problems.explanation: Inline styles (written directly on the element) take priority over styles in a stylesheet. This is a common source of confusion when AI generates inline styles that override your stylesheet. If you cannot figure out why a style is not applying, check for inline styles first.

problems.question:
AI generated a page and ALL headings are blue. You only want the main title to be blue. The CSS says:

```css
h1, h2, h3 { color: blue; }
```

What is the problem?
options: A) The selector targets all h1, h2, and h3 elements instead of just the main title
B) The `color` property does not work on headings
C) The commas in the selector are a syntax error
problems.explanation: The selector `h1, h2, h3` means "all h1 elements AND all h2 elements AND all h3 elements." That is why every heading is blue. The fix: use a class or ID to target only the main title, like `#main-title { color: blue; }` or `.page-title { color: blue; }`.

blocks.prompt: Explain in your own words what the three parts of a CSS rule are (selector, property, value) and how you would read a CSS rule out loud. Use the example `.button { background-color: green; }` in your explanation.

blocks.content: Every CSS rule follows the same pattern: selector (who), property (what), value (how). You can read any CSS rule by saying "For [selector], set [property] to [value]." CSS can live in external files, style tags, or inline on elements — and inline styles override the others. Classes (.) target groups; IDs (#) target one specific element.

blocks.prompt: Now you can read what CSS instructions say. But have you ever wondered why there is so much space around some elements and so little around others? Module 2.2 introduces the box model — the invisible boxes that surround every element on the page.

reviewQuestions.question: Every CSS rule follows the pattern:
reviewQuestions.explanation: CSS always follows: selector (who to style), then property: value pairs inside curly braces. For example: .card { color: blue; }
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: That's the pattern! Selector, property, value. That's all CSS is.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Every CSS rule is: selector { property: value; }. The selector picks which element, the property is what to change, and the value is how.

reviewQuestions.question: In CSS, a dot (.) before a name like .card means it targets:
reviewQuestions.explanation: A dot (.) targets a class. A hash (#) targets an ID. A bare name (like h1) targets an HTML tag. Classes are the most commonly used selector.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Right! Dot = class, hash = ID, bare name = tag.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: A dot (.) targets a CSS class. For example, .card matches all elements with class="card". Classes are the most common selector type.
